230227.explanation: GLPvdA supports a vital democracy with engaged citizens. However, it is important not to raise false expectations. Therefore, referenda are only possible when real choices can still be made.

230229.explanation: Housing costs in Leeuwarden are well below the national and also below the Frisian average. Levying taxes is necessary to maintain facilities for all of us.

230230.explanation: We find it reasonable that tourists also contribute to the facilities they use.

230231.explanation: The dog tax is a specific tax. We find it reasonable that people with dogs contribute to maintaining off-leash areas and facilities to counteract dog waste nuisance.

230232.explanation: Free closing times fit a student city like Leeuwarden and contribute to a gradual outflow from the hospitality industry.

230233.explanation: GLPvdA works toward a clean and healthy future for our children. Residents who contribute are financially supported.

230234.explanation: Like other urgent groups, we want to prioritize refugees for social rental housing. For example, residents from their own village also get priority. A maximum of 30% is allocated to all urgent groups.

230235.explanation: Language is essential for social participation. Therefore, GLPvdA supports free language courses.

230236.explanation: GLPvdA recognizes that seasonal workers are necessary in agriculture. We oppose the exploitation of seasonal workers and consider it important that housing meets the requirements.

230237.explanation: People are happier in a green environment. GLPvdA is greening an area equivalent to 400 soccer fields and wants to plant many new trees.

230238.explanation: A complete ban on wood stoves is legally not possible. People with lung diseases suffer greatly from wood burning. Therefore, we are considering banning wood burning in new homes. We also follow developments in Amersfoort, where a ban applies under certain weather conditions.

230239.explanation: GLPvdA wants to bring back the Warm Winter Night in Leeuwarden: a big celebration with fireworks for everyone.

230241.explanation: Frisian is part of our cultural heritage. GLPvdA encourages the use of Frisian, including in primary schools.

230243.explanation: The General Local Regulation (APV) already contains sufficient rules to regulate the number of coffee shops and any nuisance.

230244.explanation: We prefer human supervision and critically examine the use of repressive measures such as camera surveillance or preventive searches. The use of camera surveillance must be periodically reviewed to assess whether the measure is proportional.

230246.explanation: GLPvdA advocates for at least 30% social rental housing. Forty percent is not a realistic share. Housing corporations lack the financial capacity for that.

230247.explanation: GLPvdA wants to solve the housing shortage while preserving green space. If building higher is necessary for that, it can be a solution.

230248.explanation: At GLPvdA's initiative, purchase protection has been introduced. Homes can no longer be bought to rent out or speculate. This has given starters more chances on the housing market.

230249.explanation: GLPvdA wants to solve the housing shortage. Vacant offices are a good option for this. Where necessary, GLPvdA supports the social rental segment.

230251.explanation: GLPvdA recognizes the importance of work. Therefore, we support people with pathways to employment and tailored jobs (basic jobs). We oppose mandatory counter-performance as it does not work.

230252.explanation: By expanding the number of basic jobs in the neighborhood, we increase livelihood security and strengthen livability. Neighborhood resident companies play an important role in this.

230253.explanation: Research shows that people on social assistance benefits fall short every month. GLPvdA wants to support people where possible. Forgiving taxes is one of the few options.

230254.explanation: Space is limited and much of it is already used for parking. With the large housing construction task, we urgently need that space! This can be done smarter and more sustainably by, for example, creating parking facilities on the edge of the neighborhood.

230255.explanation: Paid parking in Leeuwarden is relatively cheap compared to other cities. Besides, it is an important tool to keep neighborhoods livable and to encourage cycling.

230256.explanation: We want less emissions in the city center. Therefore, we promote sustainable transport and the bundling of freight transport to the city center.

230257.explanation: GLPvdA aims for a sustainable environment. To facilitate electric driving, more charging stations are needed. We want the municipality itself to also install and operate charging stations in parking garages and at charging plazas.

230258.explanation: There is still sufficient space for parking in the villages. Paid parking is not applicable there.

230259.explanation: We want more space for greenery, pedestrians, and social encounters in the city center. In a large part of the city center, parking can be arranged within walking distance in parking garages.

230260.explanation: Good facilities and meeting places create livable neighborhoods and villages where residents know each other. In Grou and Wirdum, we want to realize a multi-functional center (MFC). The MFC in Vrijheidswijk will be renovated and strengthened. Zuidlanden and Middelsee will also get their own meeting places.

230261.explanation: In line with the established traffic policy, we want the through car traffic to disappear from Stationsweg. This will create much more space and safety for pedestrians, cyclists, and public transport.

230262.explanation: Driving faster on this road might sound attractive, but it causes noise nuisance for homes close to the road. When selling their house, residents assumed a speed of 50 km/h. With increasing traffic in this district, 50 km/h is an appropriate speed.

230263.explanation: Given the current housing shortage, GLPvdA finds it unacceptable that homes remain empty for a long time. The option of an empty property tax has been created by GL-PvdA. Locally, we want to actively investigate its introduction.

230265.explanation: GLPvdA supports the preservation of our cultural heritage. Frisian sports are part of that.

230268.explanation: GLPvdA strives for a vibrant cultural climate and a diverse events program. Arcadia is a valuable partner in this.

230270.explanation: We think it is important that every child comes into contact with art and culture multiple times. That is why we support cultural education in all schools. Free museum visits fit well with this.

230271.explanation: GLPvdA supports companies that want to increase safety. However, there is also personal responsibility involved. Financial support is not immediately evident.

230272.explanation: GLPvdA supports farmers in sustainability efforts, for example, in taking energy measures or sowing species-rich grassland.

230274.explanation: Gambling and alcohol cause much personal suffering and high societal costs. GLPvdA does not want to encourage this through advertising in public spaces.

230275.explanation: Research has shown that various groups receiving social assistance benefits can barely make ends meet. By widening income limits, we offer more residents basic livelihood security.

230277.explanation: We find social safety important. Where necessary, more street lighting can be installed, such as along the Stienzer Hegedyk cycle path. On the other hand, we also cherish the darkness in rural areas.

230278.explanation: Too many children in Leeuwarden go to school without breakfast. This negatively affects their learning performance. In addition, we support free fruit in all schools.

230279.explanation: The waste disposal tax must cover costs. By introducing pay-as-you-throw (diftar), waste separation has improved and the costs related to collection and incineration levies have decreased.

230281.explanation: GLPvdA finds it important that all residents receive good help from the municipality. Children and youth must also have a safe place to report complaints. However, this does not necessarily have to be a youth ombudsman.

230284.explanation: GLPvdA has introduced funds for children and adults so that people with a limited income can participate in sports and culture.

230285.explanation: GLPvdA supports mixed neighborhoods and villages where everyone lives together, including refugees. We invest in this if necessary.

275822.explanation: GLPvdA focuses on affordable care for the elderly and vulnerable. The municipal housing care vision outlines how this is shaped. Where necessary and possible, GL-PvdA supports this financially.

275823.explanation: GLPvdA finds it important that, besides reducing emissions, energy costs go down. Especially residents who find it difficult to pay their energy bills benefit from this. Energy coaches play a crucial role here.

275824.explanation: Making the Prins Hendrik Bridge movable will create an attractive (shorter) waterway from Dokkum to places like Warten and Grou. It will also make the city center more attractive.

275825.explanation: The municipal council has already agreed to build an asylum seekers center near the airbase. Primarily, the COA is responsible for the location choice of an asylum seekers center. The municipality checks whether it fits at this location.

275826.explanation: We believe no one in the Netherlands should have to sleep on the street. This also applies to people whose asylum applications have been rejected.

275827.explanation: We believe in providing humane shelter to people who have fled their own country. The municipal council has already approved building an asylum seekers center near the airbase.

230227.explanation: This is desirable for a consultative referendum.

230229.explanation: Yes, but only if it is responsible.

230230.explanation: This is necessary to remain/become a tourist-friendly municipality.

230231.explanation: This is an outdated tax; 240 of the 342 municipalities in the Netherlands no longer levy it.

230232.explanation: This should also be possible outside the city center ring and in the villages; we highly value free entrepreneurship. Of course, this must be in good consultation with local residents.

230233.explanation: As long as they are not in competition with municipality-affiliated energy suppliers in that area.

230234.explanation: This group should join the housing seekers queue like any other resident.

230235.explanation: Funding for this comes from The Hague; the municipality must comply with national regulations.

230236.explanation: Provided this is done in a manner that complies with regulations.

230237.explanation: As long as the open landscape character remains intact.

230238.explanation: There are national regulations for this. The RIVM's heating guide provides everyone with good advice in this matter.

230239.explanation: We believe that our residents should be consulted about this through a poll or survey.

230241.explanation: That is certainly good, but in this way we find that the people of Liwwadders are excluded; both should be addressed in primary schools.

230243.explanation: In recent years, we have not noticed any signs of expansion.

230244.explanation: We are cautious about this because privacy is a great value. We do not want American-style situations.

230246.explanation: 40% is a nice goal, but since we haven't even reached the agreed 30%, it seems wise to us to first achieve that percentage.

230247.explanation: But not in the villages without support.

230248.explanation: This is not always possible (studying children, informal care).

230249.explanation: But this also applies to other housing seekers.

230251.explanation: The Participation Act is leading in this matter.

230252.explanation: Provided there is sufficient compensation from the national government.

230253.explanation: Special social assistance is precisely intended for this purpose.

230254.explanation: There must be, or come to be, enough parking space in consultation with neighborhoods and villages.

230255.explanation: This applies only in Leeuwarden; we must not drive our tourists, passersby, and shoppers out of the city with parking fees.

230256.explanation: National or European regulations will have to provide clarity on this in the distant future.

230257.explanation: Only where there is need and demand. This also applies to public spaces.

230258.explanation: No parking meters in Grou and Stiens.

230259.explanation: It must not become a hindrance to living enjoyment and only with proper consultation and a majority vote from residents/neighbors.

230260.explanation: If there is a need, it certainly must be so.

230261.explanation: Certainly, there are far too many accidents.

230262.explanation: The structure of this road lends itself to this.

230263.explanation: What is long? And the reason must be clear.

230265.explanation: As long as enough money is invested well to keep the association running.

230268.explanation: There are also other cultural events and providers who need money.

230270.explanation: First gauge where the need lies. A free internet connection/provider or free public transport could also be an option!

230271.explanation: Then money must also be considered for the security of private property.

230272.explanation: Yes, but this must then also apply to other business sectors.

230274.explanation: Every company advertises; follow the national guidelines.

230275.explanation: No one should live in poverty.

230277.explanation: Especially if this can improve safety.

230278.explanation: We believe that in consultation with all primary schools this should be looked at; children must not go to school on an empty stomach.

230279.explanation: If the possibility exists, this should always go ahead.

230281.explanation: After a needs assessment, we are open to this; it may be possible that our child mayor can also have a role in this.

230284.explanation: The word "more" indicates that this already happens; if it proves to be too little, this will have to be reviewed. Providing extra money immediately goes too far for us.

230285.explanation: This form of housing for our Dutch youth as described is not desirable to us.

275823.explanation: This can yield financial benefits for our residents.

275824.explanation: This is not only good for our own residents but will also offer more possibilities for tourists, from which our (hospitality) entrepreneurs will also benefit.

275825.explanation: This location is absolutely unsuitable for the sheltering of refugees.

275826.explanation: The municipality of Leeuwarden must also comply with national regulations.

275827.explanation: Our municipality meets the national standard for shelter.

230227.explanation: A lower threshold ensures that residents, young and old, can have quicker and better participation. This strengthens local democracy.

230229.explanation: By making different choices about where expenditures go, the property tax (OZB) can be lowered. Landlords can pass on the OZB reduction in the rent price.

230230.explanation: Tourist tax funds pay for free facilities for tourists. Moreover, the tourist tax in Leeuwarden is already low compared to other Frisian municipalities.

230231.explanation: More and more municipalities are abolishing the dog tax because it is seen as outdated and the tax is not always clearly spent on dog facilities. Abolishing it also means savings on collection costs.

230232.explanation: Flexible closing times create more liveliness and more jobs. Furthermore, it is better for safety if not all bars close at the same time. Nuisance must be properly addressed.

230233.explanation: Support already comes from the national government and we want to support these initiatives as well. It is important that making homes sustainable becomes cheaper. Additionally, this lowers the energy costs for our residents.

230234.explanation: We do not want to discriminate between people on the waiting list. Solidarity is important, but housing shortages and waiting times are significant for everyone, especially for young people.

230235.explanation: It is also a legal obligation. It promotes integration, work, education, and reduces social problems, so as far as we're concerned, participation in language classes should be mandatory!

230236.explanation: Yes, it prevents pressure on living space. The employer remains responsible, also for tackling nuisance. The municipality can make it easier to apply for permits.

230237.explanation: We are a rural municipality and that includes having trees. However, the safety of road users remains paramount.

230238.explanation: Even in times of energy crisis, alternative heating methods must remain possible. As with everything, it is important to take neighbors into account as much as possible.

230239.explanation: The Warm Winter Night should return, especially now that there is a national fireworks ban. It is a cozy and safer alternative (especially for young people) and it causes less damage and nuisance.

230241.explanation: The "memmetaal" (frisian dialect) must be preserved, but the Liwwadders (city Frisian) should also receive more attention.

230243.explanation: Yes, but regulating is smarter than blocking. Spreading out and better rules can limit nuisance.

230244.explanation: As it is currently used, it is mainly at bottlenecks and known nuisance spots. Preferably no expansion without a clear goal, because privacy is also important.

230246.explanation: More social rental homes come at the expense of (owner-occupied) homes for other target groups (e.g., elderly). However, more attention is needed for affordable rent, especially for young people.

230247.explanation: Densification helps build homes faster, so also apartment buildings, mainly with (spacious) apartments for elderly, especially around the station and the city center.

230248.explanation: (Private) landlords also meet the need for more rental homes. Investors should, however, be kept out.

230249.explanation: Although homes are needed for all target groups, such an initiative helps students and young professionals.

230251.explanation: Those who can contribute should do so, but it must be appropriate.

230252.explanation: There is often an underlying reason behind long-term unemployment. Guidance and management must be tailored accordingly. Subsidized jobs are often not suitable for that.

230253.explanation: Forgiving debts remains necessary to prevent worse poverty. That is why we prefer to strongly focus on measures to help our residents out of poverty.

230254.explanation: In many neighborhoods there are problems due to a shortage of parking spaces. Additional spots reduce nuisance for residents. Parking close to one's own home should remain possible.

230255.explanation: Paid parking can be cheaper by using current parking facilities more efficiently and by making different choices about expenditures. Lower parking costs make the city more accessible.

230256.explanation: A ban affects entrepreneurs and supply chains. It is better to transition sustainably gradually. Furthermore, it is unnecessary since Leeuwarden's city center is one of the cleanest in the Netherlands.

230257.explanation: The energy transition goes faster if charging is easy (also in neighborhoods with few driveways). This must be aligned with the needs per neighborhood or village and should impact regular parking spots as little as possible.

230258.explanation: Villages have fewer public transport alternatives. Free parking keeps it livable. Where there is no parking pressure, the municipality should not treat parking as a cash cow.

230259.explanation: Car-free streets are pleasant for atmosphere and safety but can affect accessibility and entrepreneurs. Leeuwarden’s city center must remain accessible to residents, entrepreneurs, and people with mobility issues.

230260.explanation: Social and cultural centers promote the "mienskip" (community spirit) in neighborhoods and villages. It is a good place where young and old can meet.

230261.explanation: Current situations, like at the station and old prison, are too dangerous and confusing. Separation makes it safer.

230262.explanation: More speed also means more risk and noise. Safety comes first. Therefore, adjustments must be made.

230263.explanation: There is often a reason why homes remain empty longer. The municipality can better engage in conversations to see how it can support these owners, for example with permits.

230265.explanation: Frisian sports must be preserved; this is good for culture, but every sport should have equal opportunities to keep sports accessible for everyone.

230268.explanation: Investing in culture is important, but the costs and risks of Arcadia are disproportionately high and run into the millions. We prefer organizing events that cost less and attract a broader audience.

230270.explanation: Museums are important for the development and education of young people. This way children and youth get in touch with culture in an accessible way.

230271.explanation: Companies may also invest themselves. The municipality can support such initiatives if they benefit the public.

230272.explanation: It helps the transition, which is better than waiting or opposing it.

230274.explanation: We believe that the government should not be patronizing, although we recognize that some people can be sensitive to advertising messages.

230275.explanation: We must not increase poverty by restricting access to assistance, because then many people, including working youth, will just fall through the cracks.

230277.explanation: Yes, mainly through smart lighting that only turns on when necessary.

230278.explanation: Children learn better with a full stomach. Too many children still go to school without breakfast for whatever reason. We believe equal opportunities for a good start are important.

230279.explanation: Better waste separation has reduced waste processing costs. This should be passed on to residents and entrepreneurs through lower waste collection charges. This also makes living more affordable.

230281.explanation: The municipality must be open to complaints, even if they concern our children and youth. Young people often have less “power”; with a youth ombudsman they will be taken more seriously.

230284.explanation: Sport and culture are not a luxury. We want to prevent the exclusion of residents and youth who cannot or can only partially participate in sports and culture. Many programs already exist, but they are insufficiently known or not easy to apply for. Therefore, we also aim to increase awareness.

230285.explanation: Although mixed housing promotes integration and reduces an 'us versus them' mentality, experience has shown that such a project does not work this way in practice.

275822.explanation: We consider elderly care important and are willing to invest (extra) in it.

275823.explanation: Sustainability leads to lower energy costs and benefits everyone. Wherever the municipality can (further) assist, we should certainly do so.

275824.explanation: This creates an ideal navigation route, which is beneficial for the attractiveness of the city.

275825.explanation: No one location is ideal; each has its advantages and disadvantages. We do not support permanent asylum seeker centers.

275826.explanation: Those who are not allowed to stay here must return to their country of origin.

275827.explanation: By building one asylum seeker center (azc), we comply with the Distribution Act and fulfill our share of the national task. Therefore, no new azc needs to be added. We do not support permanent azcs.

230227.explanation: The number of signatures is an appropriate threshold. We oppose expanding referendums. We have opportunities for citizen participation. Influence on policy can come in many forms; elections remain the most important instrument.

230229.explanation: The current rate is sufficient. However, we want to waive property tax (OZB) on buildings with a public or social function (community real estate), such as community centers, sports halls, and libraries.

230230.explanation: The tourist tax can be increased. We offer sufficient quality. The municipality funds events, festivals, exhibitions, and conferences for the benefit of local businesses. Investments are discussed in consultation with the sector. It is a purpose tax, so the money stays in the right place.

230231.explanation: We finance the necessary amenities and facilities from this; for example, trash bins and off-leash areas.

230232.explanation: We advocate for a central arrangement. This is also easier to organize for enforcement and police. This contributes to safety.

230233.explanation: We encourage residents with such initiatives — from the bottom up and in cooperation.

230234.explanation: We follow the agreements made nationally. This means this group gets priority. Note: this concerns only 7% of the total supply.

230235.explanation: If this leads to better pathways to work and participation, it is important to do. Good command of the Dutch language is essential for full integration.

230236.explanation: It is an example of good employer responsibility. Employers are responsible for providing the right facilities and provisions for seasonal workers.

230237.explanation: We are in favor of greening.

230238.explanation: For many households, it is a way of heating. It also provides coziness.

230239.explanation: If we move towards banning fireworks, there must be an alternative. This can of course be arranged with a good local market party.

230241.explanation: It is a provincial responsibility. We certainly encourage Frisian and Leeuwarden dialect.

230243.explanation: We are against the expansion of coffee shops. We prefer fewer.

230244.explanation: Camera enforcement has a preventive effect. It is also a means of monitoring. We want to deploy it when the situation calls for it.

230246.explanation: The 30% target is a fine starting point. We want a good mix so that we are attractive to multiple target groups.

230247.explanation: Urban areas face a shortage of space for all functions. Economy, ecology, housing, and recreation all require space. By adding high-rise buildings, we create space for other important functions.

230248.explanation: We have a housing shortage and therefore want every home to be occupied. If space is available for recreational use, separate agreements will apply.

230249.explanation: There is a shared responsibility here. The municipality can actively contribute ideas, but developers must also be given space. Students are an important target group, but others also seek housing and must be given an opportunity.

230251.explanation: Reciprocity is very important. Someone should be able to request help from the municipality and something in return may be required.

230252.explanation: Subsidized jobs should be used when suitable for a person. They should be jobs that benefit society. It is a tool, not an obligation. Moreover, it must be affordable. We must focus the limited resources.

230253.explanation: This option should remain available, for example for the large group of working poor.

230255.explanation: We already have a low rate for our parking options. We want to keep it that way because it contributes to attractiveness and hospitality.

230256.explanation: Only when there are sufficient charging points and the infrastructure is in order can this be achieved. Consideration must be given to entrepreneurs. National guidelines are important.

230257.explanation: We facilitate applications in consultation with applicants.

230258.explanation: Many residents in rural areas still depend on their car; it is important to provide space for this.

230259.explanation: We support areas with limited car traffic, not completely car-free zones.

230260.explanation: We must assess where it is necessary and appropriate. In some cases, capacity can be consolidated. Furthermore, it is important to keep it affordable and viable.

230261.explanation: Significant investments have recently been made in the area. We must ensure optimal safety, but redeveloping is very costly.

230262.explanation: This speed limit has been agreed upon with local residents; we do not want to change it.

230263.explanation: There should be an escalation ladder. First, a conversation must take place to discuss options. A fine can be used as a last resort.

230265.explanation: Fierljeppen, skûtsjesilen, and kaatsen are very important to us. Our commitment should reflect that. Investing in sports is essential.

230268.explanation: We find it important to also invest in other forms of culture. Think of theater groups, music bands, and other events.

230270.explanation: Young people should be able to connect with art and culture in an accessible way.

230271.explanation: This is a shared responsibility of both the local government and entrepreneurs. Both will need to contribute because the interests are mutual.

230272.explanation: At our initiative, funds were reserved recently to help companies, including farmers, become more sustainable. We want to continue this.

230274.explanation: We support a healthy society and do not want to encourage gambling or drinking in any way. Young people are vulnerable to these issues and are a target group we want to monitor closely. This is a societal responsibility.

230275.explanation: There is a growing group of working poor who are struggling to make ends meet. We must be there for those in need in our municipality.

230277.explanation: Better lighting is a practical way to enhance the feeling of safety. It also improves visibility.

230278.explanation: A good breakfast contributes to children's performance. We want every child to have that. However, this should be supported by multiple parties, not just the municipality.

230279.explanation: The polluter currently pays the bill. Revenues from this cover the costs of processing.

230281.explanation: The Children's Ombudsman plays an important role in advocating for children's rights, identifying abuses, and advising governments and organizations. We must not overlook children.

230284.explanation: We encourage the Sports and Culture Fund, where residents can apply for compensation for membership fees and participation.

230285.explanation: We embrace the concept where different target groups can live together. This can be strengthening. We do not see it as the municipality's obligation to specifically steer towards these target groups.

275822.explanation: The municipality has a facilitating and stimulating role in housing development for all target groups. The elderly should have access to appropriate care based on need.

275823.explanation: The current efforts have good reach. Many people make use of them.

275824.explanation: Significant investments are planned in the maintenance of capital goods; that should be the priority. First get the basics in order before financing new expensive ambitions.

275825.explanation: The location next to the airbase is acceptable to us.

275826.explanation: National law states that asylum seekers who are not allowed to stay must leave the country after 28 days.

275827.explanation: A regional reception center is planned in Leeuwarden; the CDA supported this proposal out of compassion but always with attention to safety, participation, and integration.

230227.explanation: As VVD, we are not in favor of referendums. Complex issues are reduced to a simple YES or NO, while the matters are often far from simple. As VVD, we will of course consider the input from residents and organizations in our decisions.

230229.explanation: As VVD, we choose financial stability. We do not accept increasing local taxes such as property tax (OZB). Therefore, we will ensure these taxes remain as low as possible for residents and entrepreneurs.

230230.explanation: We want to keep Leeuwarden attractive for tourism and water recreation. We use the tourist tax directly to improve our tourist facilities, such as the Prinsentuin and Grou. Therefore, we keep the tourist tax unchanged.

230231.explanation: The dog tax is an unfair tax. Therefore, we will abolish the dog tax.

230232.explanation: We support free opening hours for our local shops and horeca. Entrepreneurs can very well decide for themselves how late they want to stay open.

230233.explanation: As VVD, we want to stimulate local energy use and production. Local energy cooperatives therefore get the space to generate and supply energy. Our municipality supports these cooperatives with local support, for example through (start-up) subsidies.

230234.explanation: As a municipality, we comply with national rules. The cabinet has decided to ban priority housing for status holders (refugees with residence permits). We will not deviate from this, so status holders will not get housing priority.

230235.explanation: Under the new integration law, municipalities are obliged to offer (free) language lessons to status holders. Leeuwarden will not deviate from this.

230236.explanation: Entrepreneurs should have the freedom to house seasonal workers themselves. If municipal rules need to be adjusted, the municipality should be accommodating. However, we do not want to force employers to house seasonal workers on their own property.

230237.explanation: In a paved environment, summer temperatures are much higher than in a green area. Following Bosk's example, we will replace paving in existing neighborhoods and villages with full greenery including trees and shrubs where possible. Safety takes priority along main roads.

230238.explanation: As VVD, we oppose nannying. Our residents can decide well for themselves whether it is sensible to light the fireplace.

230239.explanation: This year, the national fireworks ban will be in effect for the first time. It's good that a full alternative will be provided: one or more fireworks shows in the municipality, closely coordinated with local horeca. A festive start to the new year!

230241.explanation: Frysk is our second official language. It should therefore have a prominent place in education. Although the province is the supervisory body, the municipality of Leeuwarden can always encourage the use of Frysk and multilingualism.

230243.explanation: In Leeuwarden, we have established a maximum number of coffeeshops. We want to keep it that way.

230244.explanation: Many neighborhoods in Leeuwarden score poorly on safety. Camera surveillance works preventively, increases the chances of apprehension, and provides monitoring at risk locations.

230246.explanation: There is a large shortage of (affordable) housing in Leeuwarden. Introducing extra rules does not lead to extra building. We focus on building, building, building.

230247.explanation: In addition to ground-level houses, there is also a great need for apartments. Our space is limited, which means we also have to build upwards (where possible). However, it is important to provide sufficient parking facilities.

230248.explanation: Not every resident of Leeuwarden wants to buy a house. There are plenty of residents who prefer to rent and who do not qualify for social rent housing. Therefore, it must be possible to buy a house for (private) rental purposes.

230249.explanation: In recent years, several office buildings have already been converted into student housing. Developers have sufficient resources to do this themselves; no municipal funding is needed.

230251.explanation: The principle is that everyone contributes according to their ability. For those who cannot (or no longer) work, options such as a tailored workplace for people with disabilities or meaningful daily activities are explored. Therefore, we support a reciprocal obligation for social assistance recipients.

230252.explanation: As VVD, we do not believe in subsidized basic jobs. We focus on real employment. And if that step is too big, we emphasize sheltered work.

230253.explanation: Under clear conditions, we as VVD support the cancellation of municipal taxes.

230254.explanation: In various neighborhoods, it is difficult to park a car. Everyone wants to travel quickly from A to B. Many of our residents depend on a car. Therefore, we focus on creating more parking spaces.

230255.explanation: Paid parking has become more expensive from 2026. For the coming years, we will not raise parking fees. We want to abolish paid parking in new residential areas such as Middelsee.

230256.explanation: As VVD, we are against such a zero-emission zone. It drives up costs for entrepreneurs, market stall holders cannot enter the city, and livability comes under pressure. We prefer to focus on feasible and affordable alternatives.

230257.explanation: More and more residents and businesses have an electric vehicle. Therefore, the municipality must ensure a comprehensive network of charging stations. Residents who cannot charge on their own property are facilitated with so-called cable ducts so they can charge their car seamlessly from home.

230258.explanation: The village centers are under pressure: shops are vacant and the shopping center is becoming more compact. As VVD, we support livable and vital villages. Paid parking will drive visitors away.

230259.explanation: We have just begun making a number of streets car-free, such as the Eewal. Let's first properly evaluate this with residents and entrepreneurs before rolling it out further.

230260.explanation: In new residential areas, we focus not only on building homes but also on increasing livability. This requires not only sufficient space for greenery but also for facilities such as education, care, and social meeting places.

230261.explanation: We address confusing traffic bottlenecks in the municipality. Therefore, we will stop confusing shared-space areas at locations including Stationsplein, Beursplein, and Blokhuispoort.

230262.explanation: We want to travel quickly from A to B. Therefore, we will design Overijsselselaan so that 70 km/h can be driven there. At the same time, we improve access to Van der Valk.

230263.explanation: There are various cases where a home unexpectedly becomes vacant: a divorce, death, or other reasons. These people already have enough to deal with. Therefore, we will not bother them with a fine for vacancy.

230265.explanation: For us, Frisian sports such as skûtsjesilen, kaatsen, jeu de pelote, Frysk damjen and Fierljeppen are just as important as all other sports. Sport connects people, works preventively, and is fun. Therefore, every sport deserves equal funding.

230268.explanation: Arcadia and the triennials must be evaluated, and the future mandate adjusted. They cost a lot of tax money, do not reach all residents of Leeuwarden and all social layers, and deliver too little in return.

230270.explanation: Many museums are already accessible free of charge (at certain times) for our residents. Also, many schools can go to museums free via the cultural pass (CJP). Therefore, we see no need to facilitate free access with taxpayers’ money.

230271.explanation: Safety is under pressure not only in villages and neighborhoods but also on industrial sites. At night, undermining activities take place. Initiatives by companies to increase safety should be encouraged by the municipality.

230272.explanation: We encourage the sustainability of the agricultural sector. Farmers are given space to run their businesses innovatively and sustainably. We will remove restrictive regulations for using sustainable energy sources such as geothermal energy, wind energy, and other renewables.

230274.explanation: We are against patronizing our residents. Therefore, these advertisements should simply remain allowed.

230275.explanation: The way out of poverty is a paid job, not further raising the income limit. Raising the income limit will make working less rewarding. As VVD, we believe a paid job should pay more than benefits. This measure backfires.

230277.explanation: Safety is paramount! We will apply smart and sustainable lighting if needed instead of leaving neighborhoods and villages in the dark.

230278.explanation: Through the School Meals Program, schools can already offer a free breakfast. The municipality is not needed for this.

230279.explanation: As VVD, we support the principle: the polluter pays. Therefore, producing little waste should be reflected in a low waste collection charge.

230281.explanation: The municipality already has an ombudsman. This functions properly and is available for every resident, including young people.

230284.explanation: Leeuwarden is affiliated with the Adult Fund for Sport & Culture. This allows low-income residents to already receive reimbursement from the municipality.

230285.explanation: The Zembla broadcast about such an experiment in Amsterdam shows that it is far from successful. Students living in a complex with status holders feel unsafe and experience much nuisance. As VVD, we take this seriously: not in Leeuwarden.

275822.explanation: As VVD, we support more housing for the elderly: this can be in a Knarrenhof or a beautiful apartment complex. The municipality should encourage this, for example through civil-service efforts or by reducing regulations. This creates flow and accelerates housing construction.

275823.explanation: Energy coaches play an important role in supporting residents, businesses, and organizations in making their properties more sustainable.

275824.explanation: This modification and making it navigable will cost the municipality at least 9 million euros. We do not participate in money-wasting prestige projects.

275825.explanation: We do not view a reception center near the airbase as a humane place for refugees who have fled their homes. Therefore, another location must be found. As far as VVD is concerned, outside our municipal boundaries.

275826.explanation: Asylum seekers who are not allowed to stay in the Netherlands must return to their country of origin. There is no place for them in Leeuwarden.

275827.explanation: We are against establishing a regional reception center in Leeuwarden. Leeuwarden already carries a very large responsibility by accommodating asylum seekers in emergency shelters Campanille and Heliconstate.

230227.explanation: D66 supports referendums, but we believe that 300 signatures in a population of over 130,000 is already a very low threshold.

230229.explanation: D66 wants to keep the burden on residents as low as possible. Through the property tax (OZB), the municipality funds important things for residents, such as infrastructure and sports facilities. Therefore, it must be financially feasible.

230230.explanation: Tourist tax helps keep Leeuwarden affordable and attractive. Visitors help pay for the maintenance of streets, greenery, cultural venues, and events. Without sufficient revenue, residents would have to foot the bill, even though tourists benefit.

230231.explanation: More and more municipalities are abolishing the dog tax because it is no longer a purpose-specific tax. The proceeds no longer go back to dog owners. In Friesland, Leeuwarden is one of the last municipalities still charging dog tax.

230232.explanation: D66 wants more freedoms for entrepreneurs—in hospitality but also for other entrepreneurs such as supermarket owners. They know best when to serve their customers and guests.

230233.explanation: D66 wants not only financial support but also assistance with the administrative hassle for resident initiatives.

230234.explanation: We have a housing crisis in the Netherlands. Asylum seeker centers are full of people who already have a status. These status holders have been waiting for years for a house so they can move on with their lives. By continuing to give priority in Leeuwarden, we promote flow-through.

230235.explanation: Language is the key to participation in society. That is why D66 wants to invest in this area.

230236.explanation: By housing seasonal workers on their own business sites, the municipality can better enforce regulations and act against abuses. So that seasonal workers are less vulnerable and have safe and decent housing.

230237.explanation: As long as traffic safety permits, D66 wants to plant more trees.

230238.explanation: In bad weather, the disadvantages to the health of local residents outweigh the benefits to the owner.

230239.explanation: D66 wants a party at Zaailand or at Oldehoofsterkerkhof where residents can jointly celebrate the New Year. This includes a municipal fireworks show.

230241.explanation: By paying more attention to the Frisian language and culture, our second national language, we connect children with their environment and history.

230243.explanation: D66 believes that the 12 coffeeshops we currently have are sufficient.

230244.explanation: In the center of Leeuwarden and around unsafe streets (such as in the Vlietzone), we already have camera surveillance. We truly see the added value of that. But if there is no safety-related reason, we find the privacy of our residents more important.

230246.explanation: D66 supports mixed neighborhoods with social rental housing. Increasing the share of social rental to 40% can make some new construction projects unfeasible. 30% is a good mix of social and other rental and owner-occupied housing, enabling more homes to be built. This reduces waiting times for social rental housing.

230247.explanation: With high-rise buildings, we use our space efficiently. D66 believes everyone deserves an affordable and suitable home where you can live comfortably. We build more homes for one- and two-person households and student flats. In new neighborhoods and these flats, we ensure greenery and facilities.

230248.explanation: D66 Leeuwarden believes housing should be about people, not profit. Owner-occupation keeps homes affordable and neighborhoods livable. That is why we also ensured the second home ban in Warten and Grou: so homes remain available for residents who truly want to live there.

230249.explanation: D66 wants to make vacant churches and other buildings, including offices, suitable for housing. These buildings are often in attractive and centrally located places. Who wouldn’t want to live there?

230251.explanation: By contributing to the environment and society, involvement and social networks are enhanced. D66 wants to stimulate this positively, not with fines but with support. Informal care and volunteer work are (also) socially valuable work.

230252.explanation: Work pays off. It is good for the unemployed but also for our municipality. Long-term unemployed people find it difficult to get profitable work. That is why D66 wants to support employers to give the long-term unemployed a chance. Subsidies should be phased out quickly as soon as possible.

230253.explanation: D66 wants our taxes to be low and distributed as fairly as possible. However, the reality is that municipal taxes can weigh heavily on a low income. By granting exemptions, we prevent debt problems (for which the municipality is responsible).

230254.explanation: D66 wants facilities to be in order in existing and new neighborhoods. There must be space for community buildings, green areas, and many new homes. We have scarce space within the municipality, and our priority is more greenery and space for playing in the street.

230255.explanation: Parking must be affordable, also for our residents and visitors with less means. Fortunately, this is already the case for many people, especially when we compare our rates with surrounding provincial capitals.

230256.explanation: D66 wants to reduce local pollution from fine particles and other emissions. However, we must be realistic. This is not a process of a few years. At European, national, and local levels, we will work on reducing emissions and a healthy municipality.

230257.explanation: D66 strives for environmentally friendly transport, and fortunately more people are driving electric. To promote this further, D66 wants to stimulate electric shared cars and provide sufficient charging stations in every neighborhood and village.

230258.explanation: From village to shop, school, or work is often quite a trip. Therefore, the car is necessary. Visitor nuisance is less than in the neighborhoods of Leeuwarden. For a lively village, we even want to encourage visits. Where possible, people naturally park on their own property.

230259.explanation: We want the center of Leeuwarden to remain easily accessible. For this, we rely on public transport and bicycles. Car-free streets provide space for greenery and business activity. D66 finds it important that we expand this in consultation with local residents and entrepreneurs.

230260.explanation: Too often the community center only gets attention after the space is allocated and funds are spent. That must change. For new neighborhoods, a community center and/or library should be planned. Fortunately, many existing neighborhoods already have a community center. Where needed, we look for space.

230261.explanation: There is a lot of (felt) insecurity around our station. D66 wants to protect vulnerable road users. Research shows that the success of shared space depends on the context. The Stationsweg apparently is less suitable for shared space.

230262.explanation: D66 likes to keep moving. And oh, how slow it can feel to drive only 50 km/h on Overijsselselaan. Still, we are against increasing the speed. The houses in Zuidlanden were not built for the noise nuisance at that speed. Besides, the time gain is almost nil.

230263.explanation: A fine sounds quite negative. There can be many good reasons for vacancy, such as renovation. Nevertheless, we consider a vacancy tax for homeowners who unnecessarily increase housing market pressure by leaving homes vacant. We want everyone to have a chance at a nice home.

230265.explanation: All sports are important, and D66 wants to promote sports clubs and practice. We aim for more sports locations in towns and villages. Frisian sports strengthen our culture and community spirit. How wonderful if our children can experience that culture and later pass it on themselves.

230268.explanation: Arcadia strengthens our cultural identity, attracts visitors from outside the city, and stimulates the local economy. Entrepreneurs, creators, and residents benefit. D66 opts for a vibrant and creative municipality of Leeuwarden.

230270.explanation: Free access to museums for youth under 18 is a smart investment, not a cost. It sparks their curiosity, increases knowledge, and stimulates talent development. This way, a generation grows up that appreciates culture and actively contributes to our city and villages.

230271.explanation: Companies that want to secure their premises together deserve support from the municipality. It prevents problems such as theft and vandalism, keeps employees and customers safe, and makes the neighborhood more attractive to work in.

230272.explanation: Sustainability in agriculture is necessary for clean water, nature, and future-proof food. Many farmers want to but get stuck on high costs. Targeted municipal support helps accelerate this and prevents higher social costs later.

230274.explanation: Advertisements for gambling and alcohol normalize addiction and mainly affect youth and vulnerable groups. The public space belongs to all of us. By limiting these advertisements, we protect health and prevent social damage and care costs.

230275.explanation: More and more people with low to middle incomes can barely make ends meet. By raising the income threshold, we prevent debts and stress and help people in time. That is fair, effective, and saves higher healthcare and debt assistance costs later.

230277.explanation: More street lighting increases safety and accessibility outside cities and villages. With smart, energy-efficient, and targeted lighting, we limit light pollution and protect nature. This way, we make dark routes safer without unnecessary extra light.

230278.explanation: A free school breakfast ensures every child starts the day well and can concentrate better. This increases equal opportunities, relieves families, and prevents learning delays. Investing in children is priority number one for D66.

230279.explanation: Lower waste disposal charges sound appealing, but proper separation must actually pay off. In Leeuwarden, you already pay less if you separate waste well. This keeps waste processing affordable, stimulates reuse, and prevents higher costs for everyone.

230281.explanation: Children and youth need their own voice. A youth ombudsman offers a low-threshold and independent contact point, prevents problems from escalating, and helps the municipality make better policies for young residents. Investing in our children is priority number one for D66.

230284.explanation: Sports and culture are important for health, development, and social cohesion. For people with low incomes, costs are often too high. With extra support, they can participate, which increases opportunities and promotes a healthy, active, and connected society.

230285.explanation: Living together helps young people and refugees get to know each other, promotes integration, and prevents loneliness. It does require careful guidance and suitable locations. This way, we can encourage connection without compromising livability or safety.

275822.explanation: By contributing financially, we keep healthcare humane and affordable. This improves quality of life, prevents loneliness, and is cheaper than expensive care institutions. In this way, we maintain healthcare that is both humane and affordable.

275823.explanation: Energy coaches help residents gain control over their energy bills and make sustainability feasible for everyone. This saves costs, reduces CO₂ emissions, and prevents energy poverty. A smart investment that yields immediate returns for Leeuwarden.

275824.explanation: An open Prins Hendrik Bridge restores the shipping route, strengthens recreation and the water-linked economy, and makes the city more attractive. With smart technology, accessibility is maintained. This way we connect the city and water without unnecessary traffic problems.

275825.explanation: An asylum seeker center must be a safe, healthy place with peace, facilities, and good accessibility. Now that it has become clear that Defense has insurmountable objections, D66 believes we should look for another location.

275826.explanation: The municipality of Groningen already has Bed-Bath-Bread for this group of people. D66 wants to invest in status holders who are allowed to stay so that they can and want to build their future faster. There is therefore already room for this form of shelter.

275827.explanation: Leeuwarden takes responsibility for people fleeing conflict. A new asylum seeker center provides humane reception, spreads the pressure, and offers opportunities for participation and work. With good agreements and distribution, we strengthen livability and solidarity in the city. The expensive emergency reception currently costs us much more money.

230227.explanation: In our municipality, we have many accessible participation opportunities. Lowering the number of signatures required for a referendum does not contribute to increased participation.

230229.explanation: Healthy municipal finances require fair choices. The property tax (OZB) is necessary for roads, safety, and care for vulnerable people. If financially responsible, the OZB should be reduced.

230230.explanation: Tourists use facilities in the city. It is reasonable that they contribute to this via the tourist tax. This way we prevent the costs from falling entirely on residents.

230231.explanation: As a municipality, we provide facilities for dogs and their owners, but understand that the dog tax is often seen as unfair. More and more municipalities are abolishing it. We are open to reconsideration, provided the costs are fairly covered.

230232.explanation: As ChristenUnie, we support a safe and livable city center. Control over closing times is part of this.

230233.explanation: ChristenUnie encourages sustainable and local energy initiatives. Local energy cooperatives ensure that the proceeds from sustainable energy benefit their own neighborhood and not large investors. The municipality must actively support these initiatives.

230234.explanation: Housing is a fundamental right. We want to distribute homes fairly, with extra attention for vulnerable groups such as status holders. Having one’s own home helps them integrate quickly. At the same time, we acknowledge the housing shortage and seek a fair balance.

230235.explanation: Integration and participation are important. Speaking Dutch is essential for this. The municipality offers free language lessons wherever possible. Newcomers must participate seriously because free does not mean optional.

230236.explanation: Employers are responsible for good housing for foreign seasonal workers. Preferably on their own grounds, so the housing market is not additionally burdened. The most important thing is that the housing is safe and humane, regardless of the location.

230237.explanation: We focus on greening and a healthy living environment. Planting along roads is part of this. Trees improve the air, provide cooling, and help nature. This way we take good care of our environment and the future.

230238.explanation: We prioritize clean air and health. We want to reduce the use of wood stoves and open fireplaces. Freedom at home is important, but health and nuisance are also important. We choose clear rules and a gradual transition.

230239.explanation: ChristenUnie supports safe alternatives to private fireworks. A central show can provide safety and community spirit, but the municipality doesn’t necessarily have to organize it itself. It can also support citizen initiatives within the law.

230241.explanation: We support the Frisian language and culture. Frisian at school helps children connect with Fryslân and promotes language development. It is a mandatory subject and should remain so. The municipality can support and encourage schools in this.

230243.explanation: We want to combat nuisance and undermining. Too many coffeeshops do not help with this. New coffeeshops do not protect young people and normalize drug use. Livability, health, and safety are our priorities. Coffeeshops are not ordinary stores.

230244.explanation: Camera surveillance can help with safety, but always with human supervision and clear agreements. Cameras must be used in a targeted way, never replace the presence of people, and require professional use. Privacy remains an important value.

230246.explanation: We support a good mix of housing, with at least 40% social rental. Housing must remain affordable for starters, families, and people with lower incomes. More social rental helps and makes building these homes more feasible.

230247.explanation: High-rise buildings can help with the housing shortage but must fit with the environment and livability. For every residential tower, we pay attention to scale, meeting places, and neighborhood character. In some places it fits well, like Spoordok; elsewhere it fits less well.

230248.explanation: We support a self-occupancy obligation for owner-occupied homes to prevent speculation and keep homes available for residents. Houses are for living in, not for profit. Exceptions apply to immediate family such as children or parents.

230249.explanation: Empty office buildings can be converted into homes or student housing. This prevents vacancy and is sustainable. Quality and meeting spaces remain important. We assess feasibility by location and do not limit ourselves to students only.

230251.explanation: We encourage people to participate in society based on customized support and dignity, not coercion. Participation provides meaning, routine, and control. Counter-performances may be requested, but benefits must never be withheld if someone cannot provide these.

230252.explanation: Work can contribute to self-esteem. For those who find it hard to get a regular job, subsidized jobs are important. They can be a stepping stone to a regular job.

230253.explanation: We support a compassionate poverty policy. People with low incomes receive exemption from municipal taxes. This prevents them from falling deeper into debt or stress.

230254.explanation: We choose what is needed per neighborhood, with attention to accessibility, safety, greenery, and livability. Green and play spaces are more important than extra parking places. We encourage walking, cycling, public transport, and shared mobility, thereby reducing parking pressure.

230255.explanation: We balance parking fees per area against accessibility and livability. Accessibility is important, but fees help keep the city car-free. The rate must encourage shopping without filling the city up.

230256.explanation: We support an environmental zone to improve air quality in the city center. Polluting transport is banned, and entrepreneurs are encouraged to switch to clean, electric transport. Entrepreneurs receive good schemes for a smooth transition.

230257.explanation: We want electric driving to be easy for everyone. Currently, there are too few charging points. We aim for a comprehensive network of charging stations throughout the municipality.

230258.explanation: We want to keep villages attractive, accessible, and livable. The car is often necessary there. Therefore, we keep parking free to protect livability and the customer base of village shops.

230259.explanation: A car-free city center makes the city safer, more livable, and cleaner. Fewer cars are pleasant for pedestrians and terraces. We ensure that people with reduced mobility can still easily reach the city.

230260.explanation: Community centers are important places for meeting and strong communities. Not every center is well used, so together with residents, we examine per neighborhood the best and feasible form so everyone has a place to gather.

230261.explanation: Traffic safety is important, especially at the station for cyclists, pedestrians, and cars. We advocate for a good analysis and well-developed options to prevent accidents.

230262.explanation: ChristenUnie chooses traffic safety, livability, and health. Higher speeds harm the living environment and safety. The peace of residents and traffic safety are more important than time savings. 50 km/h is the safe and social standard.

230263.explanation: We actively address vacancy to reduce the housing shortage. In times of housing shortage, it is illogical that houses are empty. A fine ensures owners make their homes available again for residents.

230265.explanation: ChristenUnie wants to invest in sports and associations for everyone, not just in Frisian sports. Sports are important for health, social contacts, and community spirit. Everyone should have the opportunity to participate.

230268.explanation: ChristenUnie is critical of Arcadia but supports small arts and projects that reach a large and broad audience. We continue to invest in culture as a municipality.

230270.explanation: Culture must be accessible to all children and young people, regardless of their income. Museums are important for education and knowledge of history. By making museums free for children and young people, every child and young person in Leeuwarden gets this opportunity.

230271.explanation: The Christian Union values cooperation between entrepreneurs, the municipality, and enforcement. A safe working environment is essential for the economy. By working together with companies, the municipality can better tackle crime.

230272.explanation: Farmers are important for a sustainable future. The Christian Union supports them in transitioning to circular and nature-friendly agriculture. The municipality helps by removing obstacles and providing facilities, not just financial support.

230274.explanation: The Christian Union wants to discourage alcohol, drugs, and gambling, especially among young people and vulnerable groups. The government must protect against addiction. Advertisements for alcohol and gambling should not appear in public spaces.

230275.explanation: The Christian Union wants a compassionate poverty policy so that more people receive timely assistance. Many working people face financial difficulties. By raising the threshold, we provide a safety net for a larger group of residents.

230277.explanation: Good lighting makes cycling and walking safer, but we also pay attention to nature and the environment. Lighting is important on main routes to villages; outside those routes, it may remain dark.

230278.explanation: The Christian Union wants to fight poverty and give children equal opportunities. A free breakfast helps health and academic performance. Schools that want to offer this should be able to organize it easily. Not every school needs this.

230279.explanation: The Christian Union wants a fair waste policy that covers the costs. We invest in sustainability and waste reduction. Proper waste separation is rewarded, and the burdens on residents remain as low as possible.

230281.explanation: Children and young people must be heard and have a safe place for complaints. A youth ombudsman ensures their interests are always recognized by the municipality.

230284.explanation: Everyone should be able to participate in sports and culture. They are important for health, development, and social contacts. Money should never be a reason for exclusion.

230285.explanation: The Christian Union promotes meeting, integration, and mixed housing. This helps young people find housing faster and ensures newcomers can participate more quickly and effectively in society.

275822.explanation: The Christian Union supports building more retirement homes. Living arrangements such as courtyards help the elderly remain independent and prevent loneliness. The municipality can co-finance cases where co-funding is needed for elderly who require extra support.

275823.explanation: We must invest more in energy coaches for everyone, not only for people with low incomes. They provide practical help to reduce energy costs. This makes sustainability accessible for all households and accelerates the energy transition.

275824.explanation: We remain critical of the open navigable route. The costs are too high and the technology complex. For the Christian Union, this must not come at the expense of healthcare and social projects. Also, the accessibility of this part of the city center, including emergency services, is adversely affected.

275825.explanation: The Christian Union finds the current ROL location less suitable. Refugees need peace and safety; next to an airbase that is difficult. The municipality must seriously search for quieter locations so that a center can be realized faster and in a more humane way.

275826.explanation: Everyone deserves a roof over their head. No one may sleep on the street. Municipalities must take their responsibility and ensure humane shelter with good guidance and clear agreements. People who are not allowed to stay receive help with departure and temporary shelter as long as that is not possible.

275827.explanation: We support the arrival of the ROL and take responsibility in the shelter crisis. Leeuwarden must contribute fairly with small-scale, well-distributed shelter. This is more humane, helps integration, and creates more support in neighborhoods and villages.

230227.explanation: FVD supports direct democracy and wants power returned to the citizen. In addition to making it easier for citizens to request a referendum, FVD also supports the introduction of a local binding referendum.

230229.explanation: FVD is for tax reduction and a smaller government. We also want to stimulate home ownership, not punish it with taxes on possession.

230230.explanation: FVD supports tax reduction and wants to stimulate tourism. Tourist tax leads to higher prices for tourists and mainly affects local entrepreneurs negatively, harming the local economy.

230231.explanation: The municipality of Leeuwarden is one of the last Frisian municipalities with a dog tax. FVD wants to abolish the dog tax because it is not a targeted tax and the revenue is used for other purposes. It is also unfair compared to other pet owners and the enforcement is expensive.

230232.explanation: FVD wants to give entrepreneurs the freedom to decide when they want to open.

230233.explanation: Financial support from the municipality means payment through additional taxes. FVD is against extra taxes to finance the very expensive energy transition and useless climate measures.

230234.explanation: Giving status holders priority for housing is unfair and undesirable. Social rental homes should, according to FVD, first go to the local population, with priority for residents with local ties. FVD is also against mass migration and wants to discourage it, not stimulate it.

230235.explanation: FVD wants refugees and migrants to pay for their own language courses. This is an investment in their own future and is expected from people who come here. We are also in favor of high language and integration requirements.

230236.explanation: FVD is skeptical about broad labor migration and wants stricter selection and linking residence to work. Labor migration puts extra pressure on wages and the housing market. By letting employers take care of this themselves, this can be partially prevented.

230237.explanation: FVD plans to plant more trees for shade, cooling, and air quality, and wants to restore hedges along roads in rural areas. We strengthen the green structure in neighborhoods and rural areas. In the built-up area, greenery is maintained and kept accessible. Nature gets more space at the edges.

230238.explanation: Citizens must remain able to decide how they heat their homes. Especially now energy has become very expensive due to a failing energy transition and high energy taxes. FVD wants to focus on nuclear energy in the long term.

230239.explanation: FVD is against the fireworks ban and wants to preserve this Dutch tradition. Fireworks shows are allowed but should not come at the expense of private fireworks.

230241.explanation: FVD finds the Frisian language, culture, and history valuable and important and wants to ensure they receive sufficient attention in education, especially in primary schools.

230243.explanation: FVD supports full legalization and regulation of cannabis and the coffeeshop market so that related crime disappears. Soft drug use falls under individual freedom and personal responsibility. Leeuwarden already has many coffeeshops; no more are needed but are allowed.

230244.explanation: There is already a lot of camera surveillance in Leeuwarden, for example, in safety risk areas. More is not necessary and harms the privacy of our residents. FVD is also concerned about an expanding control system with new sensors, facial recognition technology, behavior monitoring, etc.

230246.explanation: FVD strives for a rental market where supply and demand better align, with sufficient space for mid-level rents and private rentals. Social rental housing should remain a safety net for those who need it, not a permanent dependency.

230247.explanation: FVD does not want massive apartment blocks and residential towers that impair livability or the character of the area, and which are also a less dense form of building than traditional residential blocks. For new construction, we apply traditional architecture and planning for small-scale neighborhoods.

230248.explanation: Property rights are very important to FVD. Owners may, in principle, decide for themselves what they do with their property. For example, if someone wants to buy a holiday home, this should be possible. Only in exceptional situations and in well-defined areas does FVD want to consider a self-occupancy obligation.

230249.explanation: We facilitate developers who want to transform vacant commercial and office buildings into (student) housing. This prevents deterioration, utilizes existing structures, and quickly provides housing alongside new construction.

230251.explanation: FVD sees social assistance only as a temporary safety net. A social assistance recipient must eventually transition to a full-fledged job. As long as someone receives social assistance, a counter-performance such as socially useful work is reasonable.

230252.explanation: FVD believes in full-fledged jobs. There is currently a major labor shortage, so there is no excuse for long-term unemployment.

230253.explanation: By waiving taxes and other benefits for low incomes, the municipality encourages people to keep their income low—the so-called 'poverty trap.' FVD supports simplification, reduction, and equal tax rates for everyone to make working worthwhile again.

230254.explanation: FVD supports more parking spaces in the city. We want to maintain and create parking space in (village) centers and neighborhoods so that homes, shops, and facilities remain accessible.

230255.explanation: FVD supports car ownership and free choice of mobility. The municipality should not drive away cars but facilitate them. This also includes a fair and affordable parking fee.

230256.explanation: We will not implement environmental zones, zero-emission zones, or other policy incentives that restrict (freight) automobile mobility. Choice of transport is a great good.

230257.explanation: The power grid is full due to a failing energy transition. More than 100 companies are waiting to be connected. FVD wants to prioritize this and stop the energy transition.

230258.explanation: FVD supports car ownership and free choice of mobility. The municipality should not drive away cars but facilitate them. A car is often indispensable, especially for families and working people.

230259.explanation: FVD opposes the plans to make the city center of Leeuwarden low-traffic. Accessibility by car is essential for reaching shops, businesses, and homes.

230260.explanation: FVD wants to leave this to the villages and neighborhoods themselves.

230261.explanation: 'Shared space' does not work; it is confusing and dangerous. It also obstructs traffic flow.

230262.explanation: The 'green wave' at 50 km/h causes annoyance, delays, and cars accelerating hard. Driving at 70 km/h is perfectly feasible here.

230263.explanation: To prevent vacancy, the municipality can take an active stance. But fines go too far.

230265.explanation: Frisian sports are cultural heritage and should be extra protected and encouraged.

230268.explanation: Arcadia is a left-wing subsidy sponsor.

230270.explanation: FVD finds it important that our youth develop culturally and socially. But it believes it is the parents' responsibility to make this possible.

230271.explanation: Safety in industrial areas is the responsibility of the companies.

230272.explanation: If farmers want to become more sustainable, that is allowed. But FVD opposes subsidies for sustainability.

230274.explanation: FVD is against this kind of government paternalism and believes in the personal responsibility of our citizens.

230275.explanation: The municipality already spends a lot of money on poverty assistance and combating poverty. Raising the income limit also means higher taxes, which FVD opposes.

230277.explanation: We improve lighting on poorly visible and remote routes, so residents can move safely in the evening as well. Also in and to the villages.

230278.explanation: This is the parents' responsibility. Additionally, FVD believes that the new government dietary guidelines (shifting from animal to even more plant-based foods) do not match children’s natural diet. Therefore, structural government-provided meals should be avoided.

230279.explanation: The waste collection fee must be lowered and pay-per-use (diftar) reversed. This system keeps getting more expensive and causes a lot of illegal dumping and street litter.

230281.explanation: It is important that children and youth are also heard within the municipality. A youth ombudsman could serve as a tool for this. But this does imply further government expansion, where FVD is cautious.

230284.explanation: People with low incomes already receive many benefits from the government: lower taxes, subsidies, and allowances. According to FVD, no extra money is needed here. We find this unfair to others.

230285.explanation: Recently, something similar went terribly wrong in Amsterdam. Dutch girls were massively assaulted, harassed, and even (gang) raped! FVD wants an asylum stop. No asylum seeker centers (AZC). No reception locations. And certainly no experiments risking the safety of our youth!

275822.explanation: We facilitate the construction of senior housing, life-course resistant homes, multigenerational homes, and 'knarrenhofjes' to free up family homes in existing neighborhoods.

275823.explanation: The energy transition is turning into a fiasco. On one hand, energy has become extremely expensive for our residents; on the other, the economy stalls due to grid congestion. FVD therefore wants to stop the forced energy transition. No extra energy coaches but fewer rules and lower taxes!

275824.explanation: By converting the Prins Hendrik Bridge into a movable variant, a through navigable route along the east side of the city is created. FVD supports this investment in our infrastructure. This is good for our tourism & recreation sector.

275825.explanation: FVD opposes the establishment of an asylum seeker center (ROL) in the municipality of Leeuwarden. We are against an AZC next to the airbase and against an AZC anywhere else.

275826.explanation: Rejected asylum seekers often cause extra nuisance and crime; FVD therefore does not want to accommodate them in the municipality. Asylum seekers who are not allowed to stay in the Netherlands must be deported as soon as possible.

275827.explanation: FVD opposes the establishment of an AZC in our municipality. FVD supports the preservation of our Dutch and Frisian culture and will in no way cooperate in facilitating mass migration.

230227.explanation: JA21 wants to give residents more say over decisions affecting their living environment. Referendums are an important democratic instrument. Therefore, JA21 is open to lowering thresholds. JA21 believes the outcome of a referendum should be binding.

230229.explanation: Municipal charges have become too high. JA21 wants to lower property tax (OZB) by cutting unnecessary expenses and prestige projects. Living and doing business should not be unnecessarily expensive.

230230.explanation: Tourism is important for Leeuwarden and creates jobs and liveliness. A too high tourist tax harms entrepreneurs and deters visitors. JA21 wants to reduce this tax.

230231.explanation: JA21 finds the dog tax outdated and unfair. Dog owners receive little to nothing in return. This tax, in JA21's view, can be abolished.

230232.explanation: Entrepreneurs know best what works for their business. JA21 wants to give the hospitality industry more freedom in closing hours, as long as safety and livability are not at risk. Fewer rules, more trust.

230233.explanation: JA21 believes it is not the municipality’s role to provide subsidies for initiatives to generate sustainable energy. Residents are free to take their own steps in generating sustainable energy, for example by installing solar panels on roofs.
